turns.content: Hey can you see this error in my CI log? It says 'Failed to build stage: command not found'.
Oh no, looks like a typo—maybe the Docker image name is wrong?
Yeah I tried reinstalling Docker but it still fails.
Sounds frustrating. How’s your weekend going? Got any good hiking trails?
It’s sunny, I’m thinking of hitting the lake for a kayak trip.
Nice! I’m planning to try that new Thai place on Main Street—you tried it last week?
Not yet. What did you think of the show we talked about earlier? That mystery series.
Haha, I watched two episodes yesterday; totally lost in the plot twists!
Okay, back to code: maybe I need to check if the build step has permissions.
You already tried that, right? It’s weird how Docker still can’t find the command.
I did—added it to PATH but error persists.
Hmm, could be a permission issue on the host machine. Let’s ping you after I get more logs.
Sure, thanks! Anyway, any weekend plans besides kayaking?
Just going to cook some BBQ with friends—any food hacks for grilling veggies?
I use lemon slices and olive oil; works every time.
Nice tip! I love those quick marinades.
I’ll bring the potato chips, you handle the fire.
Perfect, sounds like a good plan. See you Saturday!
Sounds great! By the way, any chance the CI will finally work this week?
If it’s anything like our
Hey, I just finished that book on event-driven architectures – it’s like our microservice architecture but with more events. Makes sense, right?
Who else has read that one? I think the analogy is cool.
Oh, only you have read it, I guess. I was thinking we could discuss it.
Actually, I haven’t finished it yet, but I’m glad you did. The book talks about…
Okay imagine two of us swapping morning routines tomorrow – I code my Python script, you set alarms. Would that work?
Haha yeah sounds chaotic but kinda fun! My alarm is at 6:30.
I read a blog about a script that auto‑gets my coffee order every morning, but I’m still figuring out the best way.
Cool! Did you try using R to pull the weather API and send a notification?
Yeah, tried an R function `weather()$temp` but got an error 'object not found'.
That’s because you called it without importing the package. Add library(weatherR) first.
I was just thinking, if we could add a function that randomly picks pizza toppings each week for the team to vote on, like `pick_toppings()`...
Cool idea! We could implement a simple script that uses `random.choice` from the standard library to pick toppings.
Yeah, but I assumed there's already a pizza‑generation API in our new codebase that just returns a JSON of toppings each day. That would be so cool.
Actually, the current codebase only has a utility function `generate_random_string()` for dummy data; there’s no pizza logic yet.
Okay, so we’d have to write one ourselves. Could we use the same repo as the weekend‑planning bot we set up last month? It already talks to Slack.
The Slack bot is just a wrapper around a Google Calendar API; it doesn’t touch pizza data at all.
Fine, let’s prototype a `pick_toppings()` that uses the current random module and logs results to a file. I’ll send you the snippet after we finish debugging the new service.
Sounds good! I’ll merge it into the shared utils folder once the build passes.
Hey, how are you doing this weekend? I’m looking to explore Rust, but honestly, why does it even work the way it does?
Oh yeah, I hear that! It’s pretty cool once you get used to it. So do you want some tips on how to start or maybe a small project idea?
Hmm, both. Maybe an RSS reader is easy enough? Like fetching feeds and parsing them.
Perfect choice! Parsing XML with Rust’s ecosystem is quite robust. You’ll probably want `reqwest` for HTTP requests and `quick-xml` or `serde_xml_rs` for deserializing the RSS feed.
Okay, so I’d use `reqwest` to fetch the feed and then parse it with `quick-xml`. Sounds right?
Yep! Here’s a quick snippet to get you started:

`use reqwest; use quick_xml::Reader;`

Could you provide the full example so I can see how it integrates?
Sure, here’s a basic skeleton:
Okay, great. So when I try to run it, I get `error[E0428]: the name `Reader` is defined multiple times` because of conflicting crates. What do I need to do?

Ah right! That’s because both `quick-xml` and `serde_xml_rs` bring their own versions of `Reader`. You should try importing a specific version of the XML crate, or use one that is more compatible with other crates. I’d recommend just sticking to `serde_xml_rs` since it integrates nicely with Serde.
